skill.name: analyzing-projects
skill.description: Guides an AI assistant through codebase discovery, including structure, technology stack, architecture, conventions, and development workflow.
skill.summary: Analyze unfamiliar projects and produce a structured technical overview.
skill.author: CloudAI-X

security_audit.summary: The static command findings are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline backticks, not Ruby backtick execution. The skill does instruct assistants to inspect files and may lead users to run package-manager commands in unfamiliar repositories, which creates a real but contextual execution risk. No evidence found of malicious intent, network exfiltration, credential harvesting, obfuscation, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Project-Provided Commands May Execute Untrusted Code
medium_findings.description: The output template lists package-manager commands such as install, development, test, and build commands. In an unfamiliar project, these commands can execute project-controlled scripts and should require user approval and sandboxing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly include npm commands and say development commands should be verified working. This is a legitimate analysis workflow, but it can execute untrusted repository code.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Repository Inspection
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes shell commands to list files and read README content. These commands are common and low impact, but they still require filesystem access to the target repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and limited to local inspection. The risk is moderate only when used against untrusted workspaces or sensitive directories.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified As Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings. Review shows these are Markdown code fences and inline code examples used to document project markers, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is Markdown documentation, not a Ruby source file or executable script. The skill contains no evidence of Ruby backtick command execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches occur in ordinary prose or template placeholders about architecture and descriptions. No evidence found of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or weak algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive Markdown text and an output template. They do not contain crypto APIs or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: README File Access Is Expected For Analysis
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding points to reading README.md during project discovery. This is expected behavior for a codebase analysis skill, but users should avoid running it in directories with unrelated sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command only reads the local README file and truncates output. It is a real filesystem action, but it is not suspicious by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Package-manager commands in the suggested report can run lifecycle scripts or arbitrary repository scripts. Assistants should ask before executing them and prefer static inspection first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the skill template and package managers commonly execute repository-controlled scripts. The skill does not require malicious execution, so this remains a contextual risk.

content.value_statement: New projects are hard to understand when structure, stack, and workflows are undocumented. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured review that produces a practical project summary.
actual_capabilities: Creates a step-by-step checklist for reviewing an unfamiliar repository.
Identifies common language, framework, package manager, and infrastructure markers.
Maps key directories and explains their likely responsibilities.
Summarizes architecture, API style, state management, database, authentication, and testing patterns when evidence exists.
Produces a structured project overview with entry points, conventions, and useful commands.
Marks uncertain observations as needing clarification.
limitations: It relies on visible repository files and may miss generated, remote, or private system details.
It does not replace a full security review or dependency vulnerability scan.
Development commands may execute project-controlled scripts and should not run without approval.
Architecture conclusions can be incomplete when documentation or source organization is sparse.

use_cases.title: Onboard To A New Repository
use_cases.description: Create a concise project map before making code changes.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineers joining an existing project

use_cases.title: Prepare Technical Documentation
use_cases.description: Convert repository evidence into a structured overview for internal docs.

use_cases.title: Assess Project Fit
use_cases.description: Identify stack, architecture, and workflow signals before deeper evaluation.
use_cases.target_user: Consultants and maintainers reviewing open source projects

prompt_templates.title: Quick Project Overview
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository at a high level. Identify the likely tech stack, main directories, entry points, and any missing evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Developer Onboarding Brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an onboarding brief for a developer. Include architecture, conventions, test workflow, development commands, and safe next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture And Workflow Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the project structure, dependency files, CI files, and source organization. Explain architecture, API boundaries, data flow, and build workflow with evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Pre-Change Repository Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Before I modify this project, analyze its structure and conventions. Highlight risks, unknowns, command execution concerns, and files I should inspect first.

output_examples.input: Analyze a new web application repository.
output_examples.output: A project summary that names the framework, package manager, key source directories, likely entry points, test setup, and open questions.

output_examples.input: Explain how this service is organized.
output_examples.output: An architecture overview describing service boundaries, API style, data layer, configuration files, and conventions supported by repository evidence.

output_examples.input: Prepare onboarding notes for a maintainer.
output_examples.output: A concise maintainer brief with setup hints, important directories, verification commands to approve before running, and areas needing clarification.

best_practices: Start with static file inspection before running any project commands.
Separate evidence-backed conclusions from assumptions or unknowns.
Ask for approval before executing package-manager, build, test, or script commands.
anti_patterns: Do not run install, build, test, or development commands in an untrusted repository without review.
Do not infer architecture from directory names alone when source evidence conflicts.
Do not include secrets, tokens, or private configuration values in the final summary.

faq.question: What does this skill help analyze?
faq.answer: It helps analyze repository structure, technology stack, architecture, conventions, entry points, and development workflow.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It suggests inspection steps, but command execution should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Can it identify every framework in a project?
faq.answer: It can identify common markers, but unusual setups or missing dependency files may require manual review.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security auditing?
faq.answer: It can surface structural risk areas, but it is not a full security audit or vulnerability scanner.

faq.question: Can it help with documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It produces structured notes that can become onboarding guides or architecture summaries.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
